agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 Annual Municipal Report presented to Council
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and a shorter Highlights version. This report is a yearly summary of the city's finances, services, and progress on goals. It is presented for Council's information and discussion, not for a final vote. Business owners should review the report to understand the city's financial health and any planned projects that could affect their operations.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Read the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and Highlights when they are posted online to stay informed about city finances and upcoming projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
2025 ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT AND ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT HIGHLIGHTS
Purpose: To present the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and 2025 Annual Municipal Report Highlights.

agenda_briefs.title: Burnaby to replace Development Procedures Bylaw, amend Building and Fees Bylaws, effective July 1, 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on repealing the current Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw (2022) and replacing it with a new Development Procedures Bylaw (2026), effective July 1, 2026. The proposal also includes amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw (2016) and the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, also effective July 1, 2026. If approved, these changes would update rules and fees for all development applications, building permits, and related processes in Burnaby, including new Development Permit types and formalized fee and refund practices.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on May 12, 2026 — if approved, the new rules and fees take effect July 1, 2026, affecting your development or building permit applications.

key_dates.label: Council vote on new Development Procedures Bylaw 2026
New Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 takes effect
Amendments to Building Bylaw and Fees Bylaw take effect

agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
PROPOSED REPEAL AND REPLACEMENT OF BURNABY DEVELOPMENT PROCEDURES BYLAW AND RELATED AMENDMENTS TO THE BURNABY BUILDING BYLAW AND BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW
Purpose:
To seek Council approval to repeal
Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw, 2022
and replace it with the proposed
Development Procedures Bylaw, 2026
, to amend the
Burnaby
Building Bylaw, 2016
and
Burnaby
Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw.

attachments.title: Proposed Repeal and Replacement of Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw and Rela

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to apply for UBCM award on development approvals
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider endorsing an application for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards. This is based on the final report for Phase 3 of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program. The DAP program aims to make the process of getting permits and approvals for building projects faster and simpler. If endorsed, the application would recognize Burnaby's work in improving how development applications are handled. This is a proposed step — no decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
DAP PROGRAM - PHASE 3 FINAL REPORT
Purpose: To provide an update on the activities and outcomes of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program and seek endorsement to apply for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for mobile devices and data plans
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services. If approved, this would authorize the city to buy new mobile phones and data plans for staff. This is a routine procurement decision and does not directly change any rules or fees for businesses.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
CA - MOBILE DEVICE AND PLANS
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on aeration and overseeding contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for aeration and overseeding services. If approved, this would mean the city hires a company to aerate (poke small holes in the soil to let air, water, and nutrients reach the roots) and overseed (spread new grass seed over existing lawns) on city-owned land. This is a routine maintenance contract and does not create new rules or fees for local businesses. The decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
CA - AERATION AND OVERSEEDING SERVICES
Purpose:
T
o obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for aeration and overseeding services.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for Victory St. road upgrades from Patterson to Jubilee
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a construction contract for road upgrades on Victory Street, between Patterson Avenue and Jubilee Avenue. If approved, this project would involve road resurfacing, sidewalk improvements, and other streetscape work. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
CA - (CB-3233) VICTORY ST. - PATTERSON TO JUBILEE ROAD UPGRADES
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Victory St. – Patterson to Jubilee Road Upgrades project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on sidewalk contract for five streets
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for new sidewalk construction on Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison streets. This is a proposed decision — if approved, the city would hire a contractor to build the sidewalks. The project is part of Burnaby's ongoing work to improve pedestrian safety and walkability. A 'contract award' means the city chooses a company to do the work and agrees to pay them. The vote has not happened yet, so the contract is not final.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.7
CA - (CA-3299) TRIUMPH, CARLETON, ROSSER, GILMORE, AND MADISON SIDEWALK PACKAGE
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison Sidewalk Package project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Contract change for Burnaby's Sidewalk Expansion Program
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change (a formal amendment to an existing agreement) for the Sidewalk Expansion Program (project EB-8022). If approved, this change would alter the terms of the contract for building new sidewalks. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CC - (EB-8022) SIDEWALK EXPANSION PROGRAM
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) to the (EB-8022) Sidewalk Expansion Program.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider comment letters on Metro 2050 land-use amendments for Maple Ridge and Surrey
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider sending comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District about two proposed changes to the Metro 2050 regional growth strategy. One change, proposed by the City of Maple Ridge, would allow development on land near North 256 Street. The other, proposed by the City of Surrey, would allow development in the Hazelmere area. These are site-specific amendments that could affect future growth and land use in those areas. Council has not yet decided what to say in the letters — they will discuss and vote on the proposed comments at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.9
COMMENT LETTERS ON PROPOSED METRO 2050 AMENDMENTS: CITY OF MAPLE RIDGE (NORTH 256 STREET) and SURREY (HAZELMERE)
Purpose:
To inform Council about two different site-specific Metro 2050 Amendments, one proposed by the City of Maple Ridge and the other by the City of Surrey, and to consider forwarding comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to review city street width standards
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a motion directing city staff to review the current street width standards. This means the city would look at how wide streets are required to be for new developments and road projects. The goal is to see if current standards are still appropriate or if changes are needed. This is a proposed review only — no new rules have been passed yet. If the review leads to changes, it could affect future construction, road design, and property development in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council's decision on this motion — if approved, a review will begin that could affect future development projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.1
COTW - PROPOSED WORKS AND SERVICES REQUIREMENTS
Purpose: To direct staff to review the City's street width standards.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 2026 consultant budget cuts
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a plan to cut consultant spending for 2026. Staff are asking for approval to prepare two lists: one showing which consultant contracts will be cut, including which city departments are affected, and another showing which consultants will keep their funding. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet. If passed, it could mean fewer outside experts hired by the city next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect city services your business relies on.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.2
FMC - 2026 CONSULTANT FUNDNG AND BUDGET REDUCTIONS
Purpose: To seek Council approval for staff to provide a comprehensive list of consultant budget cuts, including associated functions and departments, and a separate list of consultants retaining funding for 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider new rules for Burnaby Mountain trails
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan. This plan would set new rules for how trails on Burnaby Mountain are used, maintained, and protected. If approved, it could affect businesses that offer guided hikes, mountain biking tours, or other outdoor recreation on the mountain. The plan is still a draft — Council has not made a final decision yet. They are asking for public input before voting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — you may want to attend or submit comments if your business uses Burnaby Mountain trails.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.3
PRCC - DRAFT BURNABY MOUNTAIN TRAIL MANAGEMENT PLAN
Purpose: To provide an update and seek Council direction on the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider supporting Vancouver Roques Rugby's Bingham Cup 2028 bid
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a request from Vancouver Roques Rugby for official support of their bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028. The Bingham Cup is a major international rugby tournament for gay and inclusive teams. Council's support would mean the City endorses the bid, which could help the club secure the event. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet. If the bid succeeds, the tournament could bring visitors and economic activity to the region, but there is no direct cost or new regulation for local businesses at this stage.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.4
PRCC - VANCOUVER ROQUES RUGBY - BINGHAM CUP 2028 SUPPORT REQUEST
Purpose: To seek Council support for Vancouver Roques Rugby's bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue. Non-market housing means homes that are not sold at regular market prices — they are kept affordable for people with lower incomes. This is an update on the proposal, which has not yet been approved. Council will consider the project at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect nearby property values and development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
PROPOSED NON-MARKET HOUSING PROJECT AT 3961 AND 3983 NORLAND AVENUE
Purpose: To provide an update on the proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive status update on significant civic projects
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will receive a status update on significant civic projects at an upcoming meeting. This is a report-back item, not a final decision. The update will cover the progress of major city-building projects, such as new community centres, parks, and transportation infrastructure. Business owners should watch for any project delays or scope changes that could affect nearby construction, road closures, or local development opportunities.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the council meeting date and review the project update report for any impacts on your business operations or property.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.2
FMC - SU: SIGNIFICANT CIVIC PROJECTS - APRIL 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on significant civic projects.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive update on major engineering projects (Dec 2025–Apr 2026)
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a status update on major engineering projects covering December 2025 to April 2026. This is a report for information only — no bylaw or decision is being voted on at this time. The update will outline progress on key infrastructure work, but no new rules or fees are proposed. Business owners should watch for future council decisions that may follow from this update.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.3
FMC - SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on major Engineering Projects.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider status update on IT initiatives for early 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive a status update on the city's IT initiatives for January to March 2026. This is a report to keep council informed about ongoing technology projects and their progress. No new rules or fees are proposed at this time. The update is for information only and does not require a vote on new policies or spending.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.4
FMC - SU: IT INITIATIVES - JANUARY TO MARCH 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on the IT initiatives for January to March 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to consider Langara College study on inclusion in public recreation
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive an update on a research project led by Langara College that looks at how to make public recreation programs and facilities in Burnaby more inclusive, diverse, equitable, and accessible. The project is still in progress, and no decisions have been made yet. The findings could lead to future changes in how recreation services are offered to better serve Burnaby’s diverse communities.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.5
PRCC - INCLUSION, DIVERSITY, EQUITY AND ACCESSIBILITY (IDEAS) IN PUBLIC RECREATION RESEARCH IN BURNABY
Purpose: To provide an update on the IDEAs in Public Recreation research project, led by Langara College, outlining its purpose, partnership model, and progress to date, while sharing emerging insights and demonstrating how the work supports improved access, inclusion, and participation in public recreation for Burnaby’s diverse communities.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Community Safety and Well-Being Plan update for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a status update on the 2025 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan, which outlines proposed actions to improve safety and well-being in Burnaby. A 2026 update to the plan is also up for discussion. This is a proposed plan — it has not been approved yet. The report describes progress so far and what changes are being considered for next year. Business owners should watch for how new safety measures could affect their operations, such as potential new requirements for lighting, security, or community engagement.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on the 2026 update — new safety requirements could affect your business operations.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.6
PSC - SU: BURNABY COMMUNITY SAFETY AND WELL-BEING PLAN PROGRESS REPORT AND 2026 UPDATE
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on the 2025 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Burnaby Fire Department operations update to February 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will review a status update on the Burnaby Fire Department's operations through February 2026. This is a proposed update for information only — no new rules or fees are being voted on at this time. The report gives Council a snapshot of the department's recent activities, including emergency responses, training, and equipment status. Business owners should be aware that this is a routine update and does not introduce any new requirements or changes to fire safety regulations that would affect their operations.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.7
PSC - SU: FIRE DEPARTMENT (FEBRUARY 2026)
Purpose: To provide Council with the Burnaby Fire Department's status update (SU) available to the end of February 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive update on RCMP operations and annual performance plan
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will receive a status update on RCMP policing operations for December 2025 to January 2026, along with the Annual Performance Plan (APP) quarterly report for October to December 2025. This is an information report only — no decisions or bylaw changes are proposed at this time. The report gives Council a look at recent police activities and how well the RCMP is meeting its yearly goals. Business owners should be aware that this is a routine update, not a new policy or regulation.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.8
PSC - SU: RCMP STATUS REPORT (DECEMBER 2025 - JANUARY 2026) AND ANNUAL PERFORMANCE PLAN QUARTERLY REPORT (OCTOBER - DECEMBER 2025)
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on policing operations covering December 2025 to January 2026 and the Annual Performance Plan (APP) quarterly report covering October to December 2025.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 to replace 1965 version
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed new zoning bylaw that would replace the current 1965 version. The proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 would repeal the old rules and introduce new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignments. This is a major update that could affect what you can build, how you can use your property, and what permits you need. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider it at upcoming meetings.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak up about how the new rules would affect your property or business.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14815 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to repeal Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, including new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update penalty rules for zoning bylaw violations
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update how the city enforces penalties for breaking the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw. The current enforcement bylaw (from 2009) lists penalties for the old 1965 Zoning Bylaw. This amendment would replace that old list with penalties for the new 2026 Zoning Bylaw. If approved, the change would mean that anyone who violates the new zoning rules — for example, building without a permit or using a property in a way it's not zoned for — would face fines and enforcement under the updated system. The proposal is up for approval at a future council meeting and has not been passed yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — if passed, this will affect anyone who violates Burnaby's new zoning rules.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2
#14816 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 4, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (repeal Schedule A, Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 section and replace with Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to amend affordable housing definitions in fee waiver bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the definitions of 'affordable housing' and 'Zoning Bylaw' in the current Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges (ACC) and Development Cost Charges (DCC) Bylaw. Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges are fees the city charges developers to help pay for community needs like parks, daycare, and roads that come with new development. If approved, these definition changes could affect which housing projects qualify for fee waivers or reductions, potentially impacting development costs and housing affordability in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, the new definitions could affect your development project's eligibility for fee reductions.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.3
#14817 - Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges Bylaw 2024, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of ACC and DCC Bylaw 2024 (definitions of "affordable housing" and "Zoning Bylaw")

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 for redevelopment impacts
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026. This bylaw would create new rules to protect tenants who are forced to move because their building is being redeveloped (torn down and rebuilt). The proposed rules could include things like requiring landlords to give tenants more notice, help with moving costs, or offer the right to return to the new building at a similar rent. This is not law yet — it is up for discussion and a vote at upcoming council meetings.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending the public hearing to share your views on how this bylaw could affect your property or tenancy.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 — first, second, third reading vote

agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.4
#14821 - Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish protections for tenants impacted by redevelopment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to strengthen tenant protections
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009. This change is meant to improve tenant protection. If approved, the amendment would update how the city enforces rules that protect renters. The exact details of the new enforcement measures are not yet public, but the goal is to make it easier for the city to hold landlords accountable for breaking tenant protection rules. This is a proposed change only — it has not been passed yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect how tenant complaints are handled in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.5
#14822 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (tenant protection)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby mid-year fee and charge updates for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the City of Burnaby's Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw for mid-2026. This means fees for various city services, permits, and licences could change. The bylaw is called 'Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026' and is up for approval at a future council meeting. If passed, it would replace the current fee schedule with a new one for the second half of 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, check the new fee schedule for any changes to your business licences or permits.

11.1.6

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of e-bike and scooter pilot bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw amendment that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters on city streets as part of a pilot program. A 'bylaw' is a local law passed by the city. If approved, this would change the existing Street and Traffic Bylaw to permit these devices for a trial period. The decision is not yet final — Council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, the pilot program could affect how you operate your business or deliver goods.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1
#14811 - Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and scooter pilot in parks
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw amendment that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in certain Burnaby parks as part of a pilot program. The proposed change to the Parks Regulation Bylaw would create a temporary trial to see how these devices can be safely used on park paths. If approved, the pilot would set rules for where and how e-bikes and scooters can operate, and the city would collect data to decide on permanent rules later.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, the pilot will set new rules for using e-bikes and scooters in Burnaby parks.

key_dates.label: Council vote on final adoption of e-bike and scooter pilot bylaw

agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.2
#14812 - Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and e-scooter pilot bylaw amendment
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would update the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 to support a new pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, this change would allow the city to enforce rules for these devices under the existing ticketing and fine system. The pilot program itself is still being developed, and this amendment is a step to make sure the city can properly manage and enforce the rules once the pilot starts.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect how e-bikes and e-scooters are regulated and enforced in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.3
#14813 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 sets property tax rates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would set the tax rates for all taxable land and buildings in the city for the 2026 tax year. It is based on sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter, which give the city the authority to set property tax rates. If approved, this bylaw will determine how much property owners pay in municipal taxes next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.4
#14814 - Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish tax rates upon all taxable land and improvements pursuant to sections 197 and 216 of the
Community Charter

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on abandoning rezoning for 3900 Grand Promenade
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to abandon a rezoning bylaw for 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858/9898 Gatineau Place. The original rezoning, approved in September 2024, allowed two residential towers (80 and 73 storeys) with a 13-storey retail and office podium. The applicant now wants to remove the commercial office space and add 292 more market strata residential units instead. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect nearby property values and development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.3.1
#14737 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2025 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858 and 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 and 73 storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. The applicant is proposing to remove commercial office use and replace it with additional market strata residential use, which includes the proposal of 292 additional market strata units
